body_txt: Dinosaurs first appeared some 243 million years ago and dominated the Earth until roughly 66 million years ago, when an asteroid impact wiped out all but a single lineage—the birds. At their height, however, they included the largest known terrestrial herbivores and carnivores to have ever lived. In short, dinosaurs are pretty dang cool. How do you make them even cooler? By mashing them together with crocodilians—the largest carnivorous reptiles of modern times and the closest extant relatives of dinosaurs outside of birds note Which phylogenetically are dinosaurs —of course! Stemming from 19th- and early 20th-century paleoart, this trope consists of giving dinosaurs—especially theropods—and prehistoric marine reptiles crocodilian traits like exposed fangs set directly into their jaws, an exoskeleton of scutes and osteoderms, and a tail with keeled serrations (even when they don't live in the water) to make them look more ferocious, imposing, and otherwise zhuzh up their appearance. Sometimes this will be given an in-universe explanation like genetic engineering or evolution, but more often than not—especially in older works—it will not. It should be noted that while the integument of dinosaurs and marine reptiles is still largely a mystery due to the rarity of skin impressions, no known species possessed crocodilian scutes (while some dinosaurs did possess osteoderms, they were as a rule sparser and more irregularly distributed than those of crocodilians). Indeed, many species of non-avian dinosaur—especially smaller theropods like compsognathids and dromeosaurids—are now known to have been fully or partially covered in fur-like protofeathers or fully-developed pennaceous feathers, Tyrannosaurus rex is known to have possessed pebbly scales on at least some parts of its body, and marine crocodiles are known to have lost their scaly exoskeletons in order to become more hydrodynamic. This trope is often an element of the "awesomebro" paleoart trend, where artists depict dinosaurs with monstrous features such as crocodilian integument out of the belief that it makes them cooler and/or scarier than depicting them with scientifically accurate protofeathers, feathers, or smaller scales. More cartoonish examples will typically simplify the scutes into rows of spikes running down the neck, back, and tail, much like stereotypical cartoon depictions of crocodilians. A subtrope of Artistic License – Paleontology , sister trope to Goofy Feathered Dinosaur (since many recent examples can be seen as partly reactionary to this trope), and common element of Classic Carnosaurs , Dinosaurs Are Dragons , and Prehistoric Monster . Will often be a T. Rexpy , if not a straight-up Terrifying Tyrannosaur . For portrayals of that unique group of herbivorous dinosaurs that actually did have extensive osteoderms — the thyreophorans — see Tough Armored Dinosaur . For actual prehistoric crocodilians, see Colossal Croc . Some examples—particularly cartoonish ones—may be green in keeping with Green Gators .
